metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in Step 2 / AC 2 that the emitter renames `"action"` to `"kind"` in the follow-on dict AND that `test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py:297`'s assertion on `follow_on["action"]` must be updated to `follow_on["kind"]` as part of this change.
findings.finding: The plan says to emit a sentinel dict with `"kind": "create_after_hard_delete"` (Step 2), but the *existing* `apply_inbound.py:553-558` already emits a follow-on dict with key `"action": "create_after_hard_delete"` (no `"kind"` key). The plan's consumer branch checks `follow_on.get("kind") == "create_after_hard_delete"`. The plan's scope says to update the unit-test fixture for the `probe_outcome`→`reason` change, but the test at `test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py:297` asserts `follow_on["action"] == "create_after_hard_delete"` — this assertion will also break if `"action"` is renamed to `"kind"` in the emitter. The plan does not mention updating this assertion as part of the `action`→`kind` rename, leaving the test update incomplete.
findings.location: What section Step 2 / apply_inbound.py:553-558 / AC line 2 / test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py:297
findings.suggested_fix: In Step 2 add: 'Update `apply_inbound.py:555` to replace `"action": "create_after_hard_delete"` with `"kind": "create_after_hard_delete"`; update `test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py:297` to assert `follow_on["kind"] == "create_after_hard_delete"`.' In AC 2 reference both the emit-side key rename and the test assertion update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate ALL test fixtures in `test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py` (and any other test files) that pass `probe_outcome` as the branch-selector key, and include them all in the 'unit-test fixture update' scope of What #1 — not just the hard_delete fixture.
findings.finding: The `probe_outcome` → `reason` key rename in `_apply_inbound_delete` will break the redirect-branch unit-test fixture too: `test_inbound_leaf_bodies.py:333` passes `payload={'probe_outcome': 'redirect', 'new_jira_key': 'DIG-999'}`. The plan says only 'the existing unit-test fixture' for the hard_delete branch needs updating, but the same `probe_outcome` key is shared by all branch fixtures in that file (hard_delete at line 282, redirect at line 333). If the function is changed to read `reason` instead of `probe_outcome`, the redirect, out_of_window, and trash branch tests will silently fall through to the default `out_of_window` branch, producing false passes.
findings.suggested_fix: In What #1 change 'update the existing unit-test fixture (the only current caller of the hard_delete branch)' to 'update ALL unit-test fixtures that pass `probe_outcome` as a payload key in `_apply_inbound_delete` tests (hard_delete at line 282, redirect at line 333, and any others in the file) to use `reason` instead'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step to update ADR 0027/0028 (or create a new ADR/CLAUDE.md section) capturing: (a) the sentinel-dict follow-on contract shape (`kind` dispatch table in applier.py), (b) the partial-failure/rebinding recovery rule from item 7, and (c) the routing rationale (why `create_one` not `_apply_outbound_create`).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new sentinel-dict follow-on contract (`kind="create_after_hard_delete"`), a new pipeline wiring stage (follow-on consumed in applier.py and injected into the outbound batch), and a partial-failure recovery rule (item 7) — all of which extend the architectural patterns in ADR 0027/0028. None of the plan items propose updating those ADRs, creating a new ADR, or updating CLAUDE.md/design docs to capture this new pattern and its recovery contract. Future maintainers adding a third follow-on kind will have no authoritative reference for the sentinel-dict contract shape or the partial-failure lifecycle.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope target (e.g., `docs/adr/0029-hard-delete-recreate-wiring.md` or an appended section to ADR 0028) and a corresponding AC bullet: 'ADR updated/created capturing sentinel-dict follow-on contract, partial-failure recovery rule, and create_one routing rationale.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling posture for `create_one` failure in the follow-on injection path: whether it raises (aborting the pass for retry), logs-and-skips (with a metric/alert), or surfaces a partial-failure result — and add an AC covering this case.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the error-handling behavior when `create_one` itself fails (e.g. Jira API 500, network timeout, rate-limit rejection) during the follow-on injection step. It is unspecified whether a failure aborts the entire `apply()` pass, silently skips the re-create, or surfaces the error to the caller — leaving the binding in a permanently inconsistent state with no documented recovery path for this case.
findings.location: What section item 3 / AC line 2 / apply() injection point
findings.suggested_fix: Treat a `create_one` failure as a retriable pass-level error: surface the exception so the reconcile pass exits with a non-zero status (preserving the follow-on for the next pass's idempotent retry via JQL dedup), and emit a structured log/metric. Document this in item 7 alongside the existing rebind-failure case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the mechanism description in What #5: `bind_confirm` does NOT clear the old reverse entry on the same save; either (a) extend `bind_confirm` to accept an optional `old_jira_key` arg and evict it, or (b) explicitly unbind the old key before calling `bind_confirm`, and update AC item 5 to assert the old key is absent from the reverse index after re-bind.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'bind_confirm(local_id, new_key) overwrites the forward mapping; the dead old-key reverse entry is cleared on the same save' (What #5), but bind_confirm in binding_store.py:277-289 only adds the new reverse entry (self._data['reverse'][jira_key]=local_id) without removing the old one. The dead old key (e.g. DIG-OLD → local_id) persists in the reverse index until retired by note_absent→grace, not 'on the same save'. This makes the plan's mechanism claim factually incorrect, and the AC ('the dead key is not resurrected') does not test for its presence in the reverse index.
findings.suggested_fix: After `bind_confirm(local_id, new_key)`, call `self._data['reverse'].pop(old_key, None)` where old_key is the pre-delete jira_key carried in the sentinel dict. Pass the old key through the sentinel dict (add `'old_jira_key': target` when emitting in _apply_inbound_delete) so the consumer can perform the eviction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the complete canonical field list for the `create_after_hard_delete` sentinel dict (e.g., `{"kind": "create_after_hard_delete", "local_id": ..., "summary": ..., "project_key": ..., "issue_type": ..., "description": ...}`) so the consumer in `applier.py` has a deterministic contract without further investigation.
findings.finding: The sentinel dict shape is specified as `{"kind": "create_after_hard_delete", "local_id":..., ...}` where `...` is an undeclared placeholder. An executor building the consumer branch in `applier.py:469-471` must know exactly which additional fields the dict carries (e.g., summary, project_key, issue_type, description, labels) to construct the `create_one` payload — but the plan defers that decision implicitly.
findings.location: What section, bullet 2 / 3; sentinel dict shape definition
findings.suggested_fix: In bullet 2 of the 'What' section, replace `...` with the explicit field set available at the `_apply_inbound_delete` call site — at minimum the fields that `dispatch_one.create_one` requires. E.g.: `{"kind": "create_after_hard_delete", "local_id": <str>, "summary": <str>, "project_key": <str>, "issue_type": <str>, "description": <str|None>}` — state that remaining fields mirror the original inbound payload.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the single authoritative execution path for the re-create: specify whether the consumer (a) calls `dispatch_one.create_one` directly within the inbound loop (and how its result is recorded to the manifest), or (b) converts the sentinel into a properly-shaped create dict (with `action='create'`, `local_id`, `fields`) and prepends it to `outbound_list` before `_apply_batch`; and specify which fields are carried from the sentinel into the create dict.
findings.finding: The plan says the consumer in applier.py:469-471 'builds an outbound create and routes it through dispatch_one.create_one' AND separately says the creates are 'injected into the outbound batch' before _apply_batch — these are two mechanically different execution paths that produce different outcomes. Direct call to create_one in the inbound loop bypasses _apply_batch's HEAD-drift detection and manifest writing; prepending a dict to outbound_list routes through dispatch_mutation which dispatches on action='create' (not 'create_after_hard_delete'). The plan does not specify the field-mapping from sentinel dict → create dict nor which execution path is authoritative, leaving the implementer unable to produce a correct, single mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt path (b): in the applier.py inbound loop, when follow_on['kind']=='create_after_hard_delete', construct a dict {'action': 'create', 'local_id': follow_on['local_id'], 'key': follow_on.get('target',''), 'fields': <reconstructed from local tracker>} and prepend it to outbound_list. This reuses _apply_batch's drift-detection, manifest-writing, and budget-guard — all for free — while still routing through batch_dispatch.create_one (which IS dispatch_one.create_one per applier.py:181-183).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the injection mechanism in What #3: either (a) the consumer calls `dispatch_one.create_one` directly in the `applier.py` loop (before `_apply_batch`) and does NOT prepend to `outbound_list`, OR (b) converts the sentinel to a standard `{"action": "create", "fields": ..., "local_id": ...}` dict and prepends it to `outbound_list` for `_apply_batch` — pick one and remove the ambiguity.
findings.finding: What #3 says to 'prepend the converted creates to the outbound list before `_apply_batch`', but this contradicts the stated routing through `dispatch_one.create_one` rather than `_apply_batch`. The legacy `_apply_batch` path uses the `action` key to route mutations; `create_after_hard_delete` is not a registered action in `_apply_batch`'s router (only `create`, `update`, `delete`, `probe`, `conflict` exist per `reconcile.py:75-79`). The plan does not specify whether (a) the consumer calls `create_one` directly and injects the result into the outbound list, or (b) converts the sentinel to a plain `action: "create"` dict before prepending — making the injection mechanism unimplementable as written.
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer (a): in the `applier.py` inbound loop, when `follow_on.get("kind") == "create_after_hard_delete"`, call `create_one(follow_on, client=client, binding_store=binding_store, repo_root=repo_root)` directly. Do not prepend to `outbound_list` (which goes through `_apply_batch`). Document this in What #3 and AC #2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback protocol to What #3/#7 for the case where `create_one` returns `None` (budget-deferred) or raises during follow-on processing: specify whether the follow-on is silently dropped (next-pass dedup recovers), logged as a WARNING, or written back as a COMMENT on the local ticket.
findings.finding: What #7 documents only one partial-failure scenario ('re-create succeeds but rebinding fails'). The fallback for the inverse case — `create_one` itself fails (API 500, rate-limit budget exhaustion via `_REST_CALL_BUDGET`, or network error) during the follow-on injection step — is not specified. `create_one` returns `None` when budget-deferred and raises on non-retried errors; the plan does not state how the caller should handle these returns/exceptions in the new consumer branch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to What #7: 'If `create_one` returns None (budget-deferred), write a COMMENT `reconciler: re-create deferred (budget)` on the local ticket and log at WARNING; the next pass's JQL dedup will detect the absence and re-attempt. If `create_one` raises, catch, log at ERROR, write a COMMENT, and continue (non-fatal) so remaining inbound mutations are not aborted.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct Step 5's description of `bind_confirm` behaviour: it does NOT remove the old reverse-index entry on the same save; update the plan to either (a) explicitly remove `reverse[old_key]` during rebind, or (b) clearly state the stale entry lingers and is only retired via `note_absent`->grace, and tighten AC 4 to assert the stale key is absent from the reverse index (not merely 'not resurrected').
findings.finding: The plan claims that after `bind_confirm(local_id, new_key)`, 'the dead old-key reverse entry is cleared on the same save.' Reading `binding_store.py:277-289` shows `bind_confirm` adds `reverse[new_key] = local_id` but does NOT remove `reverse[old_key]`, so the stale reverse entry is NOT cleared on the same save — it lingers until `note_absent` -> grace retires it. The plan hedges this ('or, if it lingers, retired via the existing `note_absent`->grace path') but presents the same-save clear as the primary case and the test AC (AC 4) asserts 'the dead key is not resurrected' — which is true but weaker than the plan's primary claim.
findings.location: What section Step 5 / AC line 4 / binding_store.py:277-289
findings.suggested_fix: Change Step 5 to: 'After re-create, `bind_confirm(local_id, new_key)` writes the new forward and reverse entries; it does NOT remove the old reverse entry — the stale `reverse[old_key]` lingers until `note_absent`->grace retires it. The AC 4 test should assert that looking up `reverse[old_key]` either returns None or a retired entry, not `local_id`.' Optionally, add an explicit `self._data["reverse"].pop(old_key, None)` call inside `bind_confirm` if immediate cleanup is desired.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's claim that `bind_confirm` 'clears the dead old-key reverse entry on the same save' — either update `bind_confirm` to remove the prior `reverse[old_key]` entry when overwriting an existing binding, or explicitly document that the stale reverse pointer persists until `note_absent` retires it and adjust AC5's wording accordingly.
findings.finding: AC item 5 asserts 'the dead old key is not resurrected' and implies the stale reverse-index entry for the old Jira key is cleaned up 'on the same save'. However, `bind_confirm` (binding_store.py:277–289) only writes `reverse[new_key] = local_id`; it does NOT remove `reverse[old_key]`. After the re-create and `bind_confirm(local_id, new_key)`, `bindings.json` will contain both `reverse[old_key] = local_id` AND `reverse[new_key] = local_id` until `note_absent` retires the old key after 3+ consecutive 404s. The plan's statement 'cleared on the same save' is factually incorrect given the current `bind_confirm` implementation, and the AC's stronger guarantee ('dead key not resurrected') is not automatically ensured by the existing API without changes.
findings.location: What §5 ('the dead old-key reverse entry is cleared on the same save (or, if it lingers, retired via the existing note_absent->grace path)'); AC item 5 ('the dead old key is not resurrected (test)')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC and test for the `create_one`-failure path in the hard-delete re-create flow: assert that when `create_one` raises or returns an error, the reconciler logs the failure, does not corrupt the local binding, and leaves the work-item re-tryable on the next pass.
findings.finding: The plan specifies logging for create/retire success+failure and documents partial-failure recovery (re-create succeeds, rebind fails), but no test is called for the case where `create_one` itself fails (network/API error) during the hard-delete re-create flow — leaving the new user-facing flow with happy-path-only test coverage for the create step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3/AC7; What item 7 ('log create/retire success+failure')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3 or as a new AC: 'When `create_one` fails (simulated Jira API error), the follow-on is not silently dropped: the error is logged, the local_id binding is unchanged from its pre-re-create state, and no partial/corrupt sentinel remains that would prevent retry.' Wire a test using a mock that raises on `create_one` and assert those postconditions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `bind_pending(local_id)` is called in the `create_after_hard_delete` consumer branch (in `applier.py`) **before** invoking `create_one`, conforming to the existing write-ahead protocol, so `recover_pending_bindings` can rescue the binding on crash-restart.
findings.finding: The plan routes the `create_after_hard_delete` sentinel dict directly into `dispatch_one.create_one` without specifying that `bind_pending(local_id)` is called first. The existing write-ahead protocol (binding_store.py lines 8–14) requires `bind_pending → create_issue → bind_confirm → save`; `recover_pending_bindings` only scans entries in 'pending' state. If the process crashes after `create_one` succeeds but before `bindings.json` is saved, `recover_pending_bindings` will not find this `local_id` on the next pass startup, leaving an orphaned Jira issue with a planted `rebar-id:{local_id}` label but no binding record — a state that is not self-healing and constitutes data loss for the binding store.
findings.location: What §3 ('Consume it in applier.py:469-471'); AC item 3 ('Idempotent: retry after partial failure does not double-create'); What §7 ('Partial-failure: if re-create succeeds but rebinding fails...')
findings.suggested_fix: In the `applier.py` consumer branch for `kind=="create_after_hard_delete"`, call `binding_store.bind_pending(local_id)` and `binding_store.save()` before calling `dispatch_one.create_one(...)`. This puts the entry in 'pending' state so the next-pass `recover_pending_bindings` JQL scan will confirm or unbind it if the pass is interrupted after create but before final save.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the complete field vocabulary of the `create_after_hard_delete` sentinel dict (replacing the `...` placeholder with the full set of keys the consumer needs to call `create_one` with a valid payload, e.g. `fields` populated from the local ticket's last-known state or the original inbound-create payload).
findings.finding: The sentinel dict `{"kind": "create_after_hard_delete", "local_id":..., ...}` (What #2) never specifies what fields populate the `...` placeholder, yet `create_one` requires a `fields` dict containing at minimum `summary`/`title` and `issuetype`/`ticket_type` to build the Jira payload. The existing emit code at `apply_inbound.py:553-558` carries only `direction`, `action`, `target`, `local_id` — no content fields. The plan does not specify where the consumer branch in `applier.py:469-471` obtains the field content (local disk read, original mutation payload propagated through, or some other source).
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the sentinel dict definition to: `{"kind": "create_after_hard_delete", "local_id": <str>, "target": <old_jira_key>, "fields": <dict read from the local ticket's canonical YAML/JSON at the time of hard-delete>}` and add a step in What #2 that `_apply_inbound_delete` reads the local content fields before emitting the follow-on.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether dead reverse-mapping retirement is synchronous (guaranteed same `bind_confirm` save transaction) or deferred, and add a concurrency-safety note (e.g. optimistic lock, idempotency token, or 'single-writer' invariant) covering the check-then-create window in `create_one` under concurrent pass execution.
findings.finding: The retirement of the dead reverse-mapping entry after `bind_confirm(local_id, new_key)` is ambiguously specified ('cleared on the same save OR, if it lingers, retired via the existing note_absent->grace path'), and there is no concurrency guard for two simultaneous reconcile passes both detecting the same hard-delete and racing through create+bind, where the JQL dedup check-then-create window could allow both to proceed if the first create is not yet indexed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicit rationale for deferral) that exercises the `create_after_hard_delete` path against a live/staging Jira instance — e.g., a smoke test or manual verification step confirming that `dispatch_one.create_one` successfully creates a Jira issue when triggered by the new sentinel, not just that the mocked unit tests pass.
findings.finding: The E2E acceptance criterion is framed as a test ('End-to-end test: inbound hard-delete -> outbound re-create in one pass'), which in this codebase almost certainly runs against a mocked Jira client. The newly wired code path routes through `dispatch_one.create_one`, which makes live Jira API calls. No AC exercises this path against a live or staging Jira instance, and no rationale is given for deferring that exercise.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, final bullet: 'End-to-end test: inbound hard-delete -> outbound re-create in one pass'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the partial-failure recovery contract is documented (e.g., in a docstring or inline comment): 're-create succeeds but bind_confirm fails → next-pass JQL dedup prevents duplicate, no manual intervention required'.
findings.finding: Item 7 documents a partial-failure behavior ('if re-create succeeds but rebinding fails, the next pass's dedup binds to the existing issue — documented') as an explicit deliverable, but no Acceptance Criterion covers or verifies this documented recovery behavior.
findings.location: What section item 7; Acceptance Criteria (all items)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC list: '- [ ] Partial-failure recovery is documented at the call site: if re-create succeeds but bind_confirm fails, the next reconciliation pass's JQL dedup will bind to the existing Jira issue without creating a duplicate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out note to the plan: describe what an operator must do if bind_confirm has fired on a wrong re-create (e.g., manually invoke note_absent on the new key to enter the grace-retire path, or re-run with a corrected payload), making the irreversibility of bind_confirm an explicit, justified choice rather than an implicit one.
findings.finding: The `bind_confirm(local_id, new_key)` call overwrites the forward binding (destructive data mutation — the old Jira key's forward entry is permanently replaced). The plan acknowledges this and describes forward-recovery (next-pass dedup prevents a duplicate), but provides no explicit rollback or back-out path if the re-create must be undone after binding has already been overwritten.
findings.location: What section item 5; Acceptance Criteria item 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify (or replace) the new sentinel-dict injection approach in What §2-3 by comparing it to the existing typed-Mutation list-extension pattern in reconcile.py:429-443; if the sentinel approach is deliberately chosen (e.g. to avoid typed-Mutation dispatch-table coupling), state the reason explicitly.
findings.finding: The plan's core architectural decision — a new sentinel-dict follow-on kind (`create_after_hard_delete`) intercepted mid-loop in applier.py and injected into the outbound batch — is a novel pattern that competes with the codebase's existing established cross-direction follow-on mechanism (reconcile.py:429-443 builds a typed outbound Mutation and appends it to the mutation list, which is then routed normally by the dispatch table). The plan chooses the sentinel approach without justifying why it is preferable to the proven typed-Mutation list-extension pattern already used for probe→outbound follow-ons.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
